sales.title:
Paphlagonia, Sinope, AR Tetradrachm
ANCIENT GREECE. PAPHLAGONIA, SINOPE.
Silver Tetradrachm, 3rd century BC.
Obv: head of female (Sinope-Tyche?) right, wearing turreted crown. Rev: Apollo seated right on omphalos, holding kithara supported on leg; ΣINΩΠEΩN to left, A M T to right.

In secure plastic holder, graded NGC VF Strike: 5/5 Surface: 2/5, marks, certification number 2177417-001.

Rare.

Situated on the southern coast of the Black Sea, Sinope was one of the most prominent and strategically located Greek colonies in Anatolia. Originally founded in the 7th century BC by settlers from Miletos, Sinope quickly grew into a prosperous port city thanks to its excellent natural harbour and its command over maritime trade routes between Greece and the Black Sea interior. Its location also made it a key centre for contact with inland Anatolian peoples, including the Paphlagonians and later the Persians. By the Classical period, Sinope was a thriving hub of commerce, craftsmanship, and cultural exchange, issuing its own coinage and maintaining a high degree of autonomy even under foreign influence.

Sinope’s significance endured into the Hellenistic and Roman periods. It was the birthplace of the philosopher Diogenes the Cynic, whose unorthodox teachings became famous throughout the Greek world. In the 3rd century BC, it was seized by Mithradates I of Pontus, becoming a royal capital under the Pontic kings and playing a crucial role in their campaigns against Rome. The city eventually fell to Lucullus in 70 BC during the Mithridatic Wars, and was later integrated into the Roman Empire.

Reference: SNG BM Black Sea-1509; SNG von Aulock-6861; HGC 7-402; Prospero-430 = SNG Lockett-2667.
Die Axis: 11h.
Weight: 17.01 g.
